prompt: Rewrite using inversion with <Hardly>
source: She had hardly arrived when the phone rang.
correct: Hardly had she arrived when the phone rang.
explanation: Inversion with <Hardly>: <Hardly> + auxiliary + subject + past participle + <when> + past simple.

prompt: Change to subjunctive with <It is essential that>
source: He must attend the meeting.
correct: It is essential that he attend the meeting.
It's essential that he attend the meeting.
explanation: Formal subjunctive after <It is essential/important/vital that>: subject + base verb (no conjugation).

prompt: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause
source: The man who is standing by the door is my uncle.
correct: The man standing by the door is my uncle.
explanation: Reduced relative clauses: remove <who is/that is> and keep the participle.

prompt: Rewrite using inversion with <Not until>
source: I didn't realize the truth until I read the report.
correct: Not until I read the report did I realize the truth.
explanation: <Not until> + clause + inverted auxiliary + subject for emphasis.

prompt: Change to passive with <It is said that>
source: People say that he is a genius.
correct: It is said that he is a genius.
He is said to be a genius.
It is said that he's a genius.
It's said that he is a genius.
He's said to be a genius.
It's said that he's a genius.
hint: Two possible structures: <It is said that...> or <He is said to...>
explanation: Impersonal passive: <It is said/believed/reported that> or subject + <is said to> + infinitive.

prompt: Rewrite using a nominal clause with <what>
source: The thing that surprised me was his reaction.
correct: What surprised me was his reaction.
explanation: <What> replaces <The thing that> in nominal clauses for more natural style.

prompt: Rewrite using inversion with <Only after>
source: She understood the problem only after she read the full report.
correct: Only after she read the full report did she understand the problem.
Only after reading the full report did she understand the problem.
explanation: <Only after> + clause/gerund + inverted auxiliary + subject.

prompt: Change to a concessive clause with <much as>
source: I admire her talent, but I disagree with her methods.
correct: Much as I admire her talent, I disagree with her methods.
explanation: <Much as> + clause expresses concession: acknowledging something while contrasting.

prompt: Rewrite using <were it not for>
source: Without your help, I would have failed.
correct: Were it not for your help, I would have failed.
Were it not for your help, I'd have failed.
explanation: <Were it not for> is a formal inversion of <If it were not for>.

prompt: Change to emphatic form with <do/does/did>
source: I understand your concern.
correct: I do understand your concern.
explanation: Emphatic <do/does/did> + base verb adds emphasis to an affirmative statement.

prompt: Rewrite using a participle clause
source: Because he had been warned, he was careful.
correct: Having been warned, he was careful.
explanation: Perfect participle clause: <Having been> + past participle replaces <Because> + subject + past perfect passive.

prompt: Change to subjunctive with <It is vital that>
source: She must submit the report on time.
correct: It is vital that she submit the report on time.
It's vital that she submit the report on time.
explanation: Formal subjunctive: <It is vital that> + subject + base verb (no <-s>, no modal).

prompt: Rewrite using ellipsis
source: She can speak French and he can speak French too.
correct: She can speak French and so can he.
She can speak French and he can too.
explanation: Ellipsis avoids repetition: <so can he> or <he can too> replaces the repeated verb phrase.

prompt: Rewrite using fronting for emphasis
source: I have never seen such a beautiful sunset.
correct: Never have I seen such a beautiful sunset.
explanation: Fronting a negative adverb triggers inversion: <Never> + auxiliary + subject.

prompt: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (past participle)
source: The documents that were signed by the director are on the desk.
correct: The documents signed by the director are on the desk.
explanation: Reduced relative clause: remove <that were> and keep the past participle.

prompt: Change to subjunctive with <I suggest that>
source: He should apologize immediately.
correct: I suggest that he apologize immediately.
explanation: Subjunctive after <suggest>: subject + base verb (no <-s>).

prompt: Rewrite using inversion with <Little>
source: She didn't know that the letter would change her life.
correct: Little did she know that the letter would change her life.
explanation: <Little> + inverted auxiliary + subject for dramatic emphasis.

prompt: Rewrite using a participle clause (present)
source: Since she lives near the office, she walks to work.
correct: Living near the office, she walks to work.
explanation: Present participle clause: <Living> replaces <Since she lives> for conciseness.

prompt: Rewrite using ellipsis with <neither>
source: He doesn't like horror movies and she doesn't like horror movies either.
correct: He doesn't like horror movies and neither does she.
explanation: Ellipsis with <neither>: <neither> + auxiliary + subject replaces the repeated negative clause.

prompt: Rewrite using fronting with <Only when>
source: You will understand the problem only when you experience it yourself.
correct: Only when you experience it yourself will you understand the problem.
explanation: <Only when> + clause + inverted auxiliary + subject for emphasis.

prompt: Change to passive with <is thought to>
source: Experts think that the painting is a forgery.
correct: The painting is thought to be a forgery.
It is thought that the painting is a forgery.
It's thought that the painting is a forgery.
explanation: Impersonal passive: subject + <is thought to> + infinitive.

prompt: Rewrite using a nominal clause with <The fact that>
source: He lied. This disappointed everyone.
correct: The fact that he lied disappointed everyone.
explanation: <The fact that> + clause creates a nominal clause functioning as subject.

prompt: Rewrite using inversion with <In no way>
source: This decision does not reflect our values.
correct: In no way does this decision reflect our values.
explanation: <In no way> + inverted auxiliary + subject for emphatic denial.

prompt: Rewrite using a concessive participle clause
source: Although he was exhausted, he continued working.
correct: Despite being exhausted, he continued working.
Exhausted though he was, he continued working.
explanation: Concessive participle: <Despite being> + adjective, or adjective + <though> + subject + <was>.

prompt: Change to subjunctive with <It is imperative that>
source: All employees must wear safety equipment.
correct: It is imperative that all employees wear safety equipment.
It's imperative that all employees wear safety equipment.
explanation: Formal subjunctive: <It is imperative that> + subject + base verb.
